retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify medical indications for the clinical use of cocaine. Summarize the pharmacology of cocaine. Explain the toxicity of cocaine and its treatment. Review the importance of improving care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve patient outcomes for whom cocaine is a therapeutic option. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the toxicokinetics of cocaine toxicity. Describe the typical history and physical exam findings for a patient with cocaine toxicity. Summarize the management options for cocaine toxicity. Explain the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients with cocaine toxicity. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Over the past few decades, body packers have also presented to the emergency department following bag ruptures.  The other problem is that many patients have also ingested other illicit agents, including alcohol, which makes management difficult. While cocaine can adversely affect every organ in the body, its most lethal effects are on the cardiovascular system.

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine Toxicity -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The phenomenon of “unopposed alpha-stimulation,” in which blood pressure increases or coronary artery vasoconstriction worsens after the blockade of beta-2 vasodilation in cocaine-abusing patients, is controversial. This rarely-encountered adverse effect has resulted in some clinicians advocating for an absolute contraindication to the use of all beta-blockers, including specific, non-specific, and mixed. Many clinicians disregard this dogma and administer beta-blockers for cocaine-related chest pain and acute coronary syndrome, especially when there is demand ischemia from uncontrolled tachycardia. [8] Of the 1,744 total patients identified in the systematic review, only seven adverse events were from putative cases of “unopposed alpha-stimulation” due to propranolol (n=3), esmolol (n=3), and metoprolol (n=1). The authors of the original “unopposed alpha-stimulation” articles dating back to the 1980s concluded in a 2017 review that the phenomenon might be the from effects of cocaine alone, with or without beta-blockers. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Violent Patients -- Differential Diagnosis -- Medication- or Substance-Induced Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: Medication side effects: Steroids, anticholinergics Sympathomimetic use: Stimulants like cocaine or amphetamines Intoxication: Alcohol Withdrawal: Alcohol or benzodiazepine

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine Toxicity -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Over the past three decades, the rates of overdose from cocaine have gradually increased. In the United States, deaths involving cocaine range from 0.9-1.6 per 100,000 population. Only in the last five years have the rates started to decrease to 0.78 per 100,000 population. People who inject cocaine into the neck veins have been known to develop pneumothorax, thrombophlebitis, hemothorax, and myositis. In addition, there are reports that intravenous injections can be associated with aneurysms of vessels, resulting in rupture, obstruction, and fistula formation. When cocaine is combined with other illicit and prescription drugs, the mortality rates are also high. More important, cocaine and alcohol have been associated with a 16 fold increase in the risk of suicide than either agent alone. Individuals who use cocaine often select a violent method for self-harm. Finally, cocaine use during pregnancy is also associated with adverse perinatal outcomes. [14] [15] (Level V)

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine Toxicity -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the patient's presentation, laboratory testing for suspected cocaine toxicity can include a complete blood count, comprehensive chemistry panel, troponin, B-type natriuretic peptide, creatinine kinase, urinalysis, urine toxicology screen, and electrocardiogram. Imaging may also include chest x-ray, abdominal x-ray for suspected body packers or stuffers, and head CT if altered mental status suggests intracranial hemorrhage. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Addiction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine: An apparent sympathetic drive is expressed through elevated vitals. Patients are typically tachycardic and tachypneic. In addition, patients may present with acute psychosis. Intoxications may necessitate serial troponins, cardiac stress tests, and cardiac catheterization. [30] Due to the vasoconstriction of coronary vessels, troponins are regularly elevated.

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine Toxicity -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: To date, there is no drug to prevent or cure cocaine addiction. The key is to educate the patient when he or she presents to the emergency department. Patients should be urged to seek drug counseling. Those who intended to commit suicide should be referred to a mental health counselor prior to discharge.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pain (ألم بطني) is clinically associated with cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين), indicating that patients who consume cocaine may present with abdominal discomfort as a manifestation of the drug’s systemic effects. Recognizing this relationship is important for clinicians when evaluating unexplained abdominal pain, as it prompts consideration of substance use history and guides appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 92
retrieved_docs.content: the anticholinergic pharmacologic class. Collectively, these entities and relationships demonstrate that alcohol, cocaine, heroin, hallucinogens, opioids, and other psychoactive drugs are interlinked through shared diagnostic categories, dependence mechanisms, poisoning risks, and pharmacologic interventions, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment, and prevention of toxic complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with arterial erosion (تسلخ الشرايين), indicating that individuals who consume cocaine are at risk for damage to the arterial walls. This relationship highlights the vascular complications of cocaine consumption and underscores the importance of monitoring for arterial injury in patients with a history of cocaine use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1690
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine withdrawal is recognized as a diagnostic criterion within the broader category of substance‑related disorders, meaning that the presence of withdrawal symptoms after cessation of cocaine use helps clinicians identify and classify substance‑related disorders. This relationship is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use is clinically associated with both arterial erosion and increased blood pressure. The vascular damage manifested as arterial erosion reflects the harmful effects of cocaine on the arterial wall, while the stimulant properties of cocaine contribute to elevated blood pressure. Recognizing these interrelated complications is important for clinicians when evaluating patients who use cocaine, as they may require monitoring for vascular injury and hypertension and appropriate interventions to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 889
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine withdrawal is recognized as a diagnostic criterion for substance‑related disorders, indicating that the presence of withdrawal symptoms from cocaine contributes to the classification of these disorders. Substance‑related disorders are organized under the broader category of withdrawal and relapse effects, and they include the drug withdrawal syndrome (SMQ) as one of their members. The drug withdrawal syndrome (SMQ) also encompasses specific treatment approaches such as maintenance therapy for opioid substances, reflecting the role of opioid maintenance programs in managing withdrawal. Within the overarching framework of withdrawal and relapse effects, rebound tachycardia is identified as a specific physiological manifestation that can occur during withdrawal or after relapse. This interconnected structure highlights how withdrawal phenomena, diagnostic criteria, syndromic classifications, therapeutic strategies, and acute cardiovascular responses are linked in the clinical understanding and management of substance‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 32
retrieved_docs.content: بمؤثرات عقلية غير محددة** belongs to the drug‑poisoning class and includes sub‑categories **تسمم بمسكّنات تعتمد على البنزوديازيبين**, **تسمم بمسكّنات تعتمد على الفينوثيازين**, and **تسمم بمنبهات نفسية**, and that it is also a type of **سمية الكوكايين**, demonstrating that cocaine toxicity is a special case within the broader unspecified‑psychoactive‑agent toxicity. Moreover, toxicity of an unspecified, non‑medical source is linked to **سمية المعادن** (metal toxicity), illustrating the overlap between general and specialized toxic categories. Overall, the framework interconnects specific poisonings, substance‑specific mental disorders, broader toxic‑agent categories, and adverse‑care events to support clinical coding, risk assessment, and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 253
retrieved_docs.content: concepts depict a comprehensive view of substance‑related disorders, encompassing diagnostic criteria (e.g., cocaine withdrawal), various substance‑induced psychiatric and behavioral states, hierarchical classifications across languages, specific syndromes such as drug withdrawal, therapeutic approaches like opioid maintenance, and physiological sequelae such as rebound tachycardia. This integrated framework aids clinicians in recognizing the full spectrum of substance‑related conditions, their diagnostic components, and associated treatment and complication pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1855
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), abdominal pain (ألم بطني), and increased blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم). The vasoconstrictive and pro‑thrombotic effects of cocaine can precipitate acute myocardial ischemia, manifesting as unstable angina, while its sympathomimetic activity commonly raises systemic arterial pressure. Additionally, cocaine‑induced visceral vasospasm or ischemia may present as abdominal pain. Recognizing these interrelated clinical manifestations is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of patients who use cocaine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: This hierarchical knowledge base connects each causative agent to its specific poisoning type, situates those poisonings within larger accidental or unspecified psychoactive poisoning frameworks, and delineates the spectrum of organic mental disorders and intoxication syndromes that may arise, thereby aiding clinicians in agent identification, poisoning classification, and anticipation of associated neuropsychiatric manifestations for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1450
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with anemia (فقر الدم), indicating that individuals who consume cocaine may be at increased risk of developing anemia and that clinicians should be vigilant for hematologic abnormalities in this population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1813
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific subtype of فقر الدم (anemia). Clinical observations indicate that تعاطي الكوكائين (cocaine use) is associated with فقر الدم, suggesting that individuals who use cocaine may develop anemia, including acute posthemorrhagic forms. Recognizing the link between cocaine use and anemia is important for accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2465
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with several serious conditions, including unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), anemia (فقر الدم), and arterial erosion (تسلخ الشرايين). The arterial damage caused by cocaine (تسلخ الشرايين) can contribute to the development of unstable angina, while the drug’s impact on the hematologic system can lead to anemia. Anemia (فقر الدم) itself is clinically associated with a feeling of weakness (الشعور بالضعف), indicating that patients who use cocaine may experience weakness as a symptom of underlying anemia. Recognizing these interrelated complications—cardiovascular instability, vascular injury, and hematologic deficiency—is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of individuals who use cocaine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1322
retrieved_docs.content: Fenelzin is contraindicated for use with cocaine, meaning that co‑administration of fenelzin and cocaine should be avoided due to the risk of harmful drug interactions.
